title: The EndocrinologyOncology InterfaceHow Do We Help Each Other
authors: Claire Higham, DPhil, FRCP; Sacha Howell, PhD, FRCP; Peter Trainer, MD, FRCPE

items.title: Why endocrinologists are vital in cancer care
items.text: Improved survival and novel therapies increase endocrine complications; endocrinologists partner with oncologists so endocrinopathies do not delay or prohibit anticancer therapy.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "endocrinologists are vital partners to oncologists in ensuring that the endocrinopathies do not delay or prohibit anticancer therapy."

items.title: How to diagnose SIADH in a euvolemic cancer patient
items.text: SIADH requires euvolemia, serum osmolality <275 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality ≥100 mOsm/kg, urinary sodium ≥20 mEq/L, normal thyroid and adrenal function, and exclusion of drug-induced hyponatremia.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 1: "A diagnosis of SIADH requires 7 conditions to be met (in the untreated state): Clinical euvolemia • Serum osmolality <275 mOsm/kg • Urine osmolality ≥100 mOsm/kg • Urinary sodium ≥20 mEq/L"

items.title: How hyponatremia treatment depends on severity and onset
items.text: Treatment of hyponatremia depends on severity, speed of onset, and underlying diagnosis—acute life-threatening hyponatremia presents with seizures and coma.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 1: "Answer: Depends on the severity, speed of onset, and underlying diagnosis"

items.title: How to manage checkpoint inhibitor thyroiditis
items.text: Thyroiditis is often transient asymptomatic hyperthyroidism; thionamides are of no value; it usually progresses to irreversible hypothyroidism—exclude cortisol deficiency before levothyroxine.
items.reference: Thyroiditis: "Thionamide treatment (carbamazole/propylthiouracil) is of no value. Thyroiditis usually progresses to irreversible hypothyroidism. Cortisol deficiency must be excluded before levothyroxine is initiated."

items.title: Why hypophysitis requires urgent hydrocortisone when sick
items.text: Hypophysitis causes ACTH deficiency; methylprednisolone may help mass effect but is not appropriate for acute cortisol deficiency alone—physiological glucocorticoid replacement is needed.
items.reference: Hypophysitis: "Methylprednisolone is not an appropriate treatment for acute cortisol deficiency secondary to ACTH deficiency alone."

items.title: Why endocrine adverse effects should not stop checkpoint inhibitors
items.text: Early recognition and management are essential; endocrine complications are not a reason to discontinue immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
items.reference: Thyroiditis: "endocrine complications are not a reason to discontinue immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy."

items.title: How cancer therapy accelerates bone loss
items.text: Cancer therapy-related bone loss from GnRH agonists, aromatase inhibitors, antiandrogens, chemotherapy-induced ovarian failure, and glucocorticoids can be up to 7-fold faster than normal aging.
items.reference: Cancer Therapy-Related Bone Loss: "Cancer therapy-related bone loss is more rapid than postmenopausal bone loss in women or normal age-related osteoporosis in men, with rates being up to 7-fold higher than that seen with normal aging."

items.title: How to manage bone loss in young breast cancer survivor on AI
items.text: Counsel calcium 700–1000 mg and vitamin D 800 IU daily, lifestyle measures, and discuss antiresorptive risks/benefits; FRAX may not apply under age 40.
items.reference: Case Vignette 3: "Answer: Calcium, 700-1000 mg daily, and vitamin D, 800 IU daily"

items.title: Why Hodgkin survivors with chest RT have high breast cancer risk
items.text: Women treated with chest radiotherapy for childhood malignancy have very high breast cancer risk; Hodgkin survivors have ~35% breast cancer risk by age 50.
items.reference: Cancer-Related New Content Hormone Replacement Therapy: "This risk is highest in survivors of Hodgkin lymphoma, who have a 35% chance of developing breast cancer by age 50 years."

items.title: How mediastinal RT affects 10-year breast cancer risk on POP
items.text: Prior mediastinal RT gives ~15% 10-year and ~30% 20-year breast cancer risk; progesterone-only pill adds ~20% relative hazard, yielding ~18% 10-year risk.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 4: "Answer: D) 18%"

items.title: How pelvic radiotherapy causes insufficiency fractures
items.text: Radiotherapy-related insufficiency fractures occur in 10%–20% after pelvic RT; may occur with normal DXA BMD; ~83% managed conservatively with analgesia.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 5: "Radiotherapy-related insufficiency fractures occur in approximately 10% to 20% of patients who undergo pelvic radiotherapy."

items.title: How to treat radiotherapy-related sacral insufficiency fractures
items.text: Conservative management with analgesia, physiotherapy, and calcium/vitamin D repletion; bisphosphonates lack evidence; teriparatide is contraindicated after skeletal irradiation.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 5: "Answer: A, B, and C) Analgesia; physiotherapy; and calcium, 1000 mg once daily, and vitamin D, 800 IU once daily"

items.question: Euvolemic small cell lung cancer patient: Na 110, serum osm 226, urine osm 611, urine Na 42. Diagnosis?
items.explanation: Low serum osmolality with inappropriately concentrated urine and urinary sodium ≥20 in euvolemic patient meets SIADH criteria.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 1: "Answer: SIADH"

items.question: Nivolumab patient initially thought to have isolated ACTH deficiency later has high ACTH and renin with low aldosterone. Diagnosis?
items.explanation: Rising ACTH and renin with low aldosterone and bilateral adrenal PET uptake indicate primary adrenal failure.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 2: "Answer: Nivolumab-induced primary adrenal failure, as evidenced by raised ACTH and renin"

items.question: Premenopausal breast cancer survivor on goserelin and exemestane with significant BMD decline age 33→35. Initial management?
options: Calcium 700–1000 mg and vitamin D 800 IU daily with lifestyle counseling
items.explanation: Young patient without fracture: optimize calcium/vitamin D and lifestyle; discuss antiresorptives with limited young-patient data.
items.reference: Case Vignette 3: "Answer: Calcium, 700-1000 mg daily, and vitamin D, 800 IU daily"

items.question: 39-year-old Hodgkin survivor with mediastinal RT at 19 on progesterone-only pill: approximate 10-year breast cancer risk?
items.explanation: Chest RT baseline ~15% at 10 years plus POP hazard ratio ~1.2 yields ~18%.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 4: "Answer: D) 18%"

items.question: Post pelvic RT patient with sacral insufficiency fractures and normal DXA. Best treatment options?
items.explanation: Conservative analgesia, PT, and calcium/vitamin D are standard; teriparatide contraindicated after skeletal RT.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 5: "Answer: A, B, and C)"

items.question: Thyroid function during checkpoint inhibitor therapy should be checked:
options: Only at completion of all cycles
items.explanation: Guidance recommends TFT with every cycle; complications often arise within first 4 cycles.
items.reference: Thyroiditis: "Thyroid function should be checked with every cycle of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy."

items.question: For checkpoint inhibitor hypophysitis with acute ACTH deficiency without mass effect:
items.explanation: Methylprednisolone is for mass effect; ACTH deficiency requires physiological replacement.
items.reference: Hypophysitis: "Methylprednisolone is not an appropriate treatment for acute cortisol deficiency secondary to ACTH deficiency alone."

items.question: Asymptomatic checkpoint inhibitor-mediated thyrotoxicosis should:
options: Avoid thionamides; monitor for progression to hypothyroidism
items.explanation: Thyroiditis hyperthyroid phase is self-limited; thionamides do not help and may worsen subsequent hypothyroidism.
items.reference: Thyroiditis: "Thionamide treatment (carbamazole/propylthiouracil) is of no value."

items.question: When should DXA be performed in nonmetastatic cancer patients on endocrine therapy?
options: When osteoporotic fracture risk factors exist and bone-modifying therapy is considered
items.explanation: DXA of spine, hip, and femoral neck quantifies fracture risk when bone-modifying therapy is being considered.
items.reference: Bone Loss: "When 1 or more risk factor for osteoporotic fracture is present, and there is consideration for use of a bone-modifying agent, then evaluate bone mineral density"

items.question: Initial hyponatremia evaluation in cancer patients should include:
options: Serum osmolality, cortisol, thyroid function, glucose, and urine studies
items.explanation: Structured evaluation includes osmolality, adrenal and thyroid status, glucose, and urine osmolality/electrolytes.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 1: "Measurement of serum osmolality, cortisol (document exogenous glucocorticoids), thyroid function, and glucose"

items.question: Primary adrenalitis from checkpoint inhibitors may require:
items.explanation: Primary adrenal failure with low aldosterone may need fludrocortisone plus glucocorticoid replacement.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 2: "Fludrocortisone is started."

items.question: For radiotherapy-related insufficiency fractures, bisphosphonates:
options: Lack evidence of efficacy; low-turnover state may limit benefit
items.explanation: ~6% receive bisphosphonates but evidence is lacking; anabolic agents contraindicated after skeletal RT.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 5: "there is no current evidence that this is effective, particularly as there is the likelihood that this is a low bone-turnover state."

items.question: Endocrine complications of checkpoint inhibitors most commonly develop:
options: Only with CTLA4 monotherapy never PD-1
items.explanation: Most endocrine immune adverse events appear early, though they can occur later.
items.reference: Thyroiditis: "Endocrine complications most commonly develop within 4 cycles of commencing treatment."

items.explanation: Volume status assessment is central to hyponatremia differential diagnosis.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 1: "Clinical euvolemia"

items.statement: Endocrine complications are an automatic reason to permanently discontinue checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
items.explanation: Endocrine AEs should generally not lead to cessation; temporary pause if very unwell.
items.reference: Thyroiditis: "endocrine complications are not a reason to discontinue immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy."

items.statement: Thionamides are effective for checkpoint inhibitor-mediated thyroiditis.
items.explanation: Thionamides are of no value in this transient destructive thyroiditis.
items.reference: Thyroiditis: "Thionamide treatment (carbamazole/propylthiouracil) is of no value."

items.statement: Cancer therapy-related bone loss can exceed normal aging bone loss by up to 7-fold.
items.explanation: Endocrine and glucocorticoid cancer therapies accelerate bone loss markedly.
items.reference: Cancer Therapy-Related Bone Loss: "rates being up to 7-fold higher than that seen with normal aging."

items.statement: Hodgkin lymphoma survivors treated with chest RT have up to 35% breast cancer risk by age 50.
items.explanation: Highest risk among childhood chest irradiation survivors.
items.reference: Cancer-Related New Content Hormone Replacement Therapy: "survivors of Hodgkin lymphoma, who have a 35% chance of developing breast cancer by age 50 years."

items.statement: Pelvic radiotherapy insufficiency fractures can occur despite normal DXA BMD.
items.explanation: Case vignette 5 demonstrates fractures with normal hip/spine density.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 5: "they can occur in younger women with normal total hip and spine bone mineral density by DXA."

items.statement: Cortisol deficiency must be excluded before starting levothyroxine after checkpoint inhibitor thyroiditis.
items.explanation: Risk of adrenal crisis if cortisol deficiency is missed.
items.reference: Thyroiditis: "Cortisol deficiency must be excluded before levothyroxine is initiated."

items.statement: Teriparatide is contraindicated after prior radiation to the skeleton.
items.explanation: Anabolic agents contraindicated in humans with prior skeletal irradiation.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 5: "they are contraindicated in humans who have received previous radiation therapy to the skeleton."

items.assertion: Assertion: SIADH diagnosis requires inappropriately concentrated urine despite low serum osmolality.
items.reason: Reason: Because urine osmolality must be <100 mOsm/kg in SIADH.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—urine osmolality is ≥100 mOsm/kg.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 1: "Urine osmolality ≥100 mOsm/kg"

items.assertion: Assertion: Checkpoint inhibitor thyroiditis often progresses to permanent hypothyroidism.
items.reason: Reason: Because thionamides prevent progression to hypothyroidism.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—thionamides are of no value.
items.reference: Thyroiditis: "Thyroiditis usually progresses to irreversible hypothyroidism."

items.assertion: Assertion: High-dose methylprednisolone is inappropriate for isolated ACTH deficiency without mass effect.
items.reason: Reason: Because methylprednisolone is inferior to physiological replacement for acute cortisol deficiency alone.
items.explanation: Both true per chapter guidance.
items.reference: Hypophysitis: "Methylprednisolone is not an appropriate treatment for acute cortisol deficiency secondary to ACTH deficiency alone."

items.assertion: Assertion: Cancer survivors on aromatase inhibitors are at risk for accelerated bone loss.
items.reason: Reason: Because endocrine therapy never affects bone mineral density.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—AI/GnRH/antiandrogen therapy accelerates loss.
items.reference: Cancer Therapy-Related Bone Loss: "added risks of treatment-related bone loss due to hypogonadism from endocrine therapy"

items.assertion: Assertion: Progesterone-only contraception may further increase breast cancer risk after chest RT.
items.reason: Reason: Because POP increases breast cancer hazard while on therapy with HR ~1.2.
items.explanation: Both true—combined risk yields ~18% at 10 years in vignette.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 4: "progesterone-only contraception increases breast cancer risk while on therapy with a hazard ratio of approximately 1.2."

items.assertion: Assertion: Most radiotherapy-related pelvic insufficiency fractures are managed conservatively.
items.reason: Reason: Because approximately 83% are managed conservatively with analgesia.
items.explanation: Both true per meta-analysis cited.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 5: "83% are generally managed conservatively with analgesia (Answer A)"

items.assertion: Assertion: Thyroid function should be checked with each checkpoint inhibitor cycle.
items.reason: Reason: Because endocrine complications never occur after cycle 4.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—most common within 4 cycles but monitoring continues.
items.reference: Thyroiditis: "Thyroid function should be checked with every cycle of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy."

items.assertion: Assertion: Nivolumab can cause primary adrenal failure with elevated ACTH and renin.
items.reason: Reason: Because isolated ACTH deficiency always presents with low ACTH.
items.explanation: Assertion true for revised diagnosis; reason false—primary failure raises ACTH.
items.reference: Clinical Case Vignette 2: "Answer: Nivolumab-induced primary adrenal failure, as evidenced by raised ACTH and renin"
